canto_title: The Marble Gallery and the Contorted Souls

scenes.summary: The gate of the lustful closes behind the travelers, making the crooked path appear straight.
The poets pass through a moving stone that oscillates like a wave.
Virgil advises using skill to navigate the shifting movement of the stone.
The poets emerge from the narrow passage onto a vast, solitary, and desolate plain.
Dante describes the immense, enclosing scale of the marble ledge surrounding them.
The marble ledge is revealed to be adorned with carvings so fine they surpass nature itself.
A beautiful carving of the Annunciation is described, capturing the moment of Mary's 'Fiat'.
Virgil instructs Dante not to fix his attention on just one single spot.
Dante moves around Virgil to view a different historical story carved into the opposite side of the rock.
Dante observes a carving of the Ark being pulled by oxen, accompanied by a choir of alternating voices.
Dante notices a carving of Michal portrayed as a sorrowful and spiteful woman.
A grand carving depicting the Emperor Trajan and his victorious Roman forces is revealed.
A dialogue is depicted in the stone between a grieving widow and a divine figure regarding justice and duty.
Dante reflects on the many wonders of the carvings while greedily seeking out new sights.
The poet pauses to warn the reader not to be dismayed by the severity of the coming punishment.
Dante expresses confusion at the movement of the figures, and Virgil directs him to look beneath the stones.
Dante delivers a stern rebuke to prideful Christians, comparing them to larvae that fail to become butterflies.
Dante compares the contorted souls to architectural corbels that bear heavy weight with strained limbs.
The scene concludes by describing how the intensity of the souls' physical compression correlates to their level of patience.
